schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch and Ellicott fix the frontier sites (Shochoh, Ephes-dammim, the 'boundary of blood') and read the chapter as the compiler's turn to David's rise.
Gill: the Philistines struck on hearing of Saul's melancholy, thinking it their chance to avenge the last defeat.
Wesley and Clarke: they had learned Samuel had forsaken Saul; Clarke dates the war some eight years after David's anointing.
Henry: Israel's ground would never have footed Philistine armies but for unfaithfulness; God suspends the judgment and ripens the giant for destruction.
Keil and Delitzsch map the terebinth valley (Emek ha-Elah) with its deeper brook-cutting between the hosts; Clarke glosses Elah as the terebinth or oak valley.
Gill: Saul, recovered by David's music, is roused by the invasion to muster his forces.
Clarke supplies the meaning of the valley's name and its well-known site.
Gill reports Kimchi's and Vatablus's reconciliations of encamping 'in the valley' yet standing 'on the mountain.'
Keil and Delitzsch distinguish the broad plain from the narrow gully; the armies hold the flanking slopes with the brook between.
Clarke: two eminences from which the hosts could see and speak to one another.
Keil and Delitzsch render ish habbenayim the 'middle-man' who decides a war by single combat, and (with Thenius) reckon the height about nine feet two.
Gill: a 'middle person' or dueller who offered to decide the war by two champions.
Clarke surveys the versions and computes nine feet nine to nearly twelve feet; Wesley settles for 'at least nine feet nine,' not unparalleled among the ancients.

debates.question: How tall was Goliath, and does the text stand?

sides.position: about nine to eleven feet, following the Hebrew 'six cubits and a span'
'four cubits and a span' (near eight feet), the reading of the Septuagint and Josephus

schools.summary: Ellicott reads a 'breastplate of scales,' the sea-people imitating a fish's coat; Keil and Delitzsch describe the brazen helmet and corselet.
Gill: brass plates laid over one another like a fish's scales, the whole a piling-up of natural advantage.
Clarke and Wesley agree on scale armour but reckon the weight differently, by different values of the shekel.

debates.question: What did the coat of mail weigh?

sides.position: about 156 pounds, rating the shekel by Cumberland's tables
about 78 pounds, on the common shekel of a quarter-ounce

schools.summary: Gill: brass greaves, boot-like, reckoned from Homer at some thirty pounds.
Clarke: greaves commonly worn on one leg (Vegetius), and the kidon distinguished from the shield, elsewhere rendered a spear.
Keil and Delitzsch take kidon as a lance, defending 'between the shoulders' from Bochart against Thenius's emendation to a shield.

debates.question: What was the brazen object 'between his shoulders'?

sides.position: a lance or slung javelin
a small shield (Septuagint, Vulgate, Luther; Thenius's emendation)

schools.summary: Gill: the spear proportionally some twenty-six feet, against Hector's sixteen and a half in Homer.
Keil and Delitzsch: shaft like a weaver's beam, iron head about seventeen pounds (cf. the parallels in Samuel and Chronicles); a shield-bearer went before him.
Clarke totals the armour near 273 pounds against Plutarch's ordinary 60; Wesley caps the antiquarian comparisons with Pliny's Athanatus.

themes: the spear like a weaver's beam

schools.summary: Ellicott presses 'Am not I THE Philistine?' and asks whether the challenge was timed to news that the Spirit had left Saul; Keil and Delitzsch paraphrase the whole taunt.
Gill: the giant's voice, strong as his frame, rose to the battalions on the hill.
Clarke gives the Targum's expansion — Goliath boasting he slew Eli's sons and took the ark.
Gill: the giant had no authority to pledge his people, nor did the Philistines abide by his terms when he fell.
Clarke: 'of this stipulation we hear nothing farther' — a boast dressed as a treaty.
Keil and Delitzsch give the plain terms; Ellicott sets single combat in its ancient world, recalling the colloquies of the Iliad.
Gill: 'I reproach' — Goliath would upbraid Israel with cowardice for finding no champion.
Clarke: ani cheraphti, 'I strip and make bare,' set among Hindoo and Homeric pre-battle abuse.
Keil and Delitzsch: the mockery lay in calling Israel mere 'servants of Saul'; the daily defiance ripens his pride.

themes: the armies of the living God

schools.summary: Ellicott marks the absence of prayer and of the Urim, and the strange contrast with former Hebrew daring: an evil spirit is on the king.
Clarke: they saw no man able; Wesley: Jonathan sits still because unstirred by God — the bravest are only what God makes them.
Henry: the followers are cowards because the leader is; the Spirit having departed, one Philistine's words make Saul change colour.
Henry: David's return to the sheep after court is a rare humility — 'before honour is humility.'
Ellicott defends the passage as genuine, best read as a parenthesis; the Septuagint omitted it from difficulty.
Clarke, with Kennicott and Pilkington, doubts vv.12-31 as a later interpolation absent from the Vatican Septuagint; Wesley: Jesse was old, so went not himself.
Gill supplies only the bare identification of David as Jesse's son.

debates.question: Are vv.12-31 (with 55-58) genuine, given the Septuagint omission?

sides.position: genuine repetition drawn from contemporary documents
a later rabbinic interpolation, absent from the Vatican Septuagint

schools.summary: Gill: the three eldest were forward and zealous to follow Saul in defence of king and country.
Keil and Delitzsch defend the pluperfect grammar and note the name-variants of Shammah across Samuel and Chronicles.
Gill: the genealogy is given for David's sake, the youngest, who will fill the camp and court.
Keil and Delitzsch: David was 'going and returning,' not in permanent service, at home with the sheep when the Philistines mustered.

themes: the divine choice of the least

schools.summary: Ellicott: the compiler inserts this verse to show he knew David had already served as court musician — the books drawn from prophetic-school documents.
Gill: 'from above Saul'; recovered by the music, David was dismissed, going and coming as need required.
Wesley: David went from court, permitted home, to be sent for again upon occasion.
Gill: the Jews held the giant timed his challenge to the hours of the Shema and prayer, an assault on Israel's worship.
Keil and Delitzsch: the forty days are the deadlock's measure, into which David is dispatched with provisions.

outliers.note: Following Wordsworth and Augustine, reads the 'forty days' as a type of Christ's forty days of temptation — 'in David is Christ.'

schools.summary: Gill: Jesse had elder sons at home, yet providence picks David for the errand, 'there being work for him to do Jesse knew nothing of.'
Keil and Delitzsch read the commission as wholly practical — parched grain and loaves, sent quickly to sons on campaign.
Gill (with Jarchi): the captain of the thousand may be Jonathan; the cheeses a gift to secure good usage.
Clarke: eastern 'cheese' is lightly pressed, salted curds carried in rush baskets.
Keil and Delitzsch: 'take their pledge' means bring back a token that the brothers are alive and well.

outliers.note: Carries Wordsworth's typology: David sent from Bethlehem to brethren who ill-receive him, as Christ was sent and 'his own received him not' (John 1:11).

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch and Ellicott: the verse is part of Jesse's own direction, not a narrator's parenthesis — 'they are in the valley of the Terebinth.'
Wesley: 'fighting' means in readiness to fight, the armies drawn up rather than engaged.
Gill identifies 'they' as the sons of Jesse.

themes: keeping the reader in the tent

schools.summary: Henry: up early yet leaving the sheep with a keeper — faithful in little, fit to rule over much.
Ellicott and Keil and Delitzsch: David reached the wagon-rampart just as the army raised the war-cry and the hosts drew up.
Wesley: Jesse little thought of the timing, but the wise God orders every circumstance to his own glory.
Gill: both sides drew up in line of battle.
Keil and Delitzsch read the clause as the charged setting into which David runs.
Henry: both sides now prepared to fight, so David's arrival at this juncture bears every mark of design.
Gill: David left the provisions with the baggage-keeper, the brothers being ready to engage.
Ellicott glosses 'carriage' as baggage (cf. Isaiah, Acts); Keil and Delitzsch: he ran into the ranks to greet his brothers.
Henry: diligent in his business, David is 'in the way of preferment; he shall stand before kings' (Prov. 22:29).
Gill: David was talking with his brothers when the champion drew near.
Keil and Delitzsch correct a Hebrew slip in 'ranks'; Ellicott raises the difficulty of the giant's name over against 2 Samuel 21:19.

debates.question: How is the 'Goliath' here related to the Goliath slain by Elhanan (2 Samuel 21:19)?

sides.position: 'Goliath' was a general name for the Gath giants
the name belongs to the giant Elhanan slew and was transferred in error (Ewald); this champion is usually called simply 'the Philistine'

schools.summary: Gill: they fled even before hearing him, knowing his words by forty days' repetition.
Ellicott: the panic 'reads like a page out of the story of another race' — Saul's torpor settled on his soldiers; Keil notes the collective noun.
Wesley: one Philistine could not rout ten thousand unless their Rock, forsaken, had 'sold' them (Deut. 32:30).
Keil and Delitzsch: Saul probably made the promise but did not feel bound; Ellicott canvasses the sense of 'free.'
Wesley: 'free' means exempt from the tributes and charges of court and camp.
Gill: the soldiers rehearse the royal bounty to one another.

debates.question: What is meant by making the victor's house 'free in Israel'?

sides.position: exemption from personal service, taxes, and war-contributions (the Corvée)
elevation to noble rank as 'freeholders' (Ewald), a step between king and subject

schools.summary: Henry: two things fired David — an uncircumcised stranger, and the armies of the living God whose affront reflects on God himself; yet he keeps his modesty (Prov. 25:6).
Keil and Delitzsch: his words hint he means to fight; the idolater 'has to do with a living God' (Berleburger Bible); Ellicott paints the fervent shepherd going group to group.
Gill: David spoke to the men standing next him, within hearing of the giant.
Gill: the people told him the honour proposed for the giant's slayer.
Keil and Delitzsch: the people's repeated assurance argues that Saul had really made the promise.
Henry: David asks 'some' and then 'others' not from ambition but that his indignation might be reported to Saul.
Henry: Eliab's abuse is jealousy — he would rather Goliath triumph than David; 'who can stand before envy' (Prov. 27:4; 18:19).
Keil and Delitzsch: Eliab 'sought the splinter in his brother's eye'; Ellicott recalls the remembered anointing that fed the elder's envy.
Wesley glosses 'naughtiness' as false-confidence and vain curiosity, exclaiming at the folly of envy.

themes: the projection of a proud heart

schools.summary: Clarke: 'I have spoken but a word'; Wesley reads a spark of faith — 'is this giant invincible? is our God unable?'
Ellicott gives both renderings; Keil and Delitzsch take the modest 'it was only a word,' shaming the reprover.

debates.question: How should David's reply be rendered?

sides.position: 'It was only a word' — deprecating his brother's anger
'Is there not a cause?' — justifying his holy zeal

schools.summary: Gill: David turned from Eliab to another, undeterred by the rebuke.
Keil and Delitzsch: the repeated inquiry is the channel by which David's words reach Saul.
Wesley: he tarried, 'secretly moved by God's Spirit,' speaking with many that it might come to the king's ear.
Gill: David's words passed 'through many hands' until they came to Saul.
Keil and Delitzsch: told to Saul, David is sent for; Ellicott: much more was said, David feeling the Spirit's strength from his anointing (16:13).
Henry: a little shepherd has more courage than all the mighty men — God does great things by the weak.
Gill: David perceived all hearts had failed, and (Kimchi) said it partly within himself.
Keil and Delitzsch render the offer plainly; Clarke notes the verse connects straight with v.11.
Clarke ties the verse to v.11, the ground of his interpolation theory.
Henry and Wesley: he says 'let no man's heart fail,' not 'thy heart,' to spare the king — a shepherd braver than all Israel.

themes: the weak things of the world

schools.summary: Gill: Saul objects that David has neither the strength nor the skill for such a warrior.
Keil and Delitzsch summarize the exchange; Ellicott: Saul, half in admiration, doubts yet is disposed to grant the petition.
Clarke reckons David about 22-23, Wesley 'not above twenty,' a raw soldier.

sides.position: about twenty-two or twenty-three
not above twenty, a novice

themes: faith versus the arithmetic of war

schools.summary: Gill: David's answer rests on experience and fact, proving he was not so inexpert as Saul supposed.
Keil and Delitzsch parse the article and the grammar of lion and bear; Ellicott hears a narrative 'bearing the stamp of truth,' with an invisible Guardian.
Clarke lays out David's reasons as a numbered argument of tried courage and divine help.
Henry: the shepherd careful for one lamb is fit to be king and a type of Christ the good Shepherd (Isa. 40:11).
Gill: the mention of the beard suggests a lion; David snatched the lamb from its mouth and slew the beast.
Clarke: the lion and the bear were slain on two different occasions, each a separate proof of courage.
Gill: 'both the lion and the bear' denotes many, killed at different times — a settled experience of victory.
Ellicott: lions and bears were common in wooded Palestine; the idolater 'has to do with a living God, not a lifeless idol' (Berleburger Bible).

themes: Goliath as a beast to be slain

schools.summary: Gill: David ascribes his victories not to strength or skill but to the power of God.
Keil and Delitzsch ground his courage in faith that the living God will not let his people be defied; Ellicott hears in Saul's blessing the old, better king.
Wesley: the beasts threatened only his sheep, this Philistine God's people; Clarke: Saul saw reasonable grounds and wished him success.
Henry: experiences are our encouragements — 'he that has delivered does and will' (cf. 2 Tim. 4:17).
Gill: not Saul's own worn armour but spare gear, and ill-suited to David's different frame.
Clarke: prudent means though the battle is the Lord's; Wesley: military vestments 'such as buff-coats are now.'
Ellicott: Saul, omitting no earthly means, loads David with the best array, too heavy and cumbersome for one slighter than the king.
Gill: Saul perhaps furnished the sword too, completing the borrowed array.
Keil and Delitzsch: David tried to walk, found he could do nothing, and took up staff, stones, and sling.
Clarke: heavy armour needed long training; Wesley: 'I have no skill in the management of such arms.'
Henry: not how excellent a thing is but how proper; this was of the Lord, that the victory might plainly be by faith.
Gill: David chose the shepherd's habit and staff over a soldier's.
Henry: no armour but his shepherd's coat and sling — his confidence purely in the power of God, who put it into his head with what weapons to fight.
Clarke: the sling gave him a rifle's advantage over a spear; Wesley: contemptible arms chosen for skill, assurance, and God's greater honour.

outliers.note: Following Wordsworth and Augustine, reads the five smooth stones as the five books of Moses, with which the greater David laid Satan low, replying 'It is written.'

schools.summary: Gill: the giant came on by slow paces under his weight, 'with a haughty air and a proud gait.'
Keil and Delitzsch: 'closer and closer,' the shield-bearer going before.
Wesley: a signal that the challenge was accepted; Clarke refers back to the great shield of v.7.
Henry: as if distrusting his own armour, a man went before him carrying his shield.
Gill: Goliath looked for a man like himself, saw a puny youth, and took it as an affront.
Clarke: from David's ruddy complexion he supposed him effeminate; Wesley: he lacked even a martial countenance.
Keil and Delitzsch render the contemptuous 'sticks' and the curse by Israel's God.
Henry: the giant disdained him lest so mean a champion should lessen the glory of his victory.
Gill: David thought him little better than a dog, for his barking blasphemy.
Clarke: Goliath likely never saw the coiled sling, and 'prayed his gods to curse' David, as in Homeric and Hindoo abuse.
Ellicott and Keil and Delitzsch: 'by his God,' the idolater using the sacred Name, thus defying the Eternal of Hosts.

debates.question: Did Goliath curse David 'by his gods' or 'by his God'?

sides.position: by his heathen gods
by Israel's own God, using the sacred Name in defiance

schools.summary: Gill: the giant stood still, disdaining to close with so slight a foe, and bade David come up.
Clarke: he meant, once he laid hold of him, 'to pull him to pieces.'
Ellicott sets the boast beside Hector's threat to Ajax in the Iliad.
Clarke: what Goliath expected from his arms, David expected from the ineffable Name.
Keil and Delitzsch hear 'bold, believing courage': David comes in the name of the Lord of Sabaoth whom the giant defied.
Henry unfolds the double title — Lord of all hosts, and God of Israel's armies, pledged to their defence (cf. Ps. 20:7).
Gill: David answers the contempt and the threats with the Name.

themes: the Name of the Lord of hosts

schools.summary: Gill: David could name the particulars only by divine inspiration.
Keil and Delitzsch give the sweep of the prediction; Ellicott: the victory is God's, whose name Goliath had just abused.
Clarke: a direct, circumstantial prophecy; Wesley: 'that God, the only true God, is for Israel.'
Henry: David seeks not his own honour but that all the world should know there is a God in Israel.
Gill: 'this assembly' is the congregation and church of the living God, spectators of the event.
Clarke: 'it is the Lord's war'; Wesley: he can save without arms, the events of war wholly in his power.
Ellicott hears the day's lesson echoed in the Psalter (Ps. 44:6-8; 33:16-20).
Henry: David goes 'rather as a priest offering sacrifice' than a soldier; the Lord saves not with sword and spear (Ps. 46:9).
Henry: the giant advanced 'like a stalking mountain,' David 'hasted and ran, lightly clad' — 'he knew whom he had believed.'
Wesley: David ran, 'so far was he from fear'; Clarke marks the end of the parley.
Gill: the giant arose in wrath, which hastened him to the encounter.
Gill: David slung the stone and smote the Philistine.
Keil and Delitzsch: the stone entered his temples; Ellicott: the face, normally covered by the borne shield, was left open by the giant's contempt (cf. Judg. 20:16).
Clarke defends the sling's force from Diodorus and the Baleares; Wesley: the proud giant had lifted his visor 'by the singular direction of providence.'
Henry: to the one open spot 'not so much David's art as God's providence directed the stone.'
Gill: David prevailed with sling and stone only, 'without any other warlike weapon.'
Keil and Delitzsch: the historian's summary — stronger 'without a sword in his hand'; Ellicott dwells on how narrow the margin was, to magnify the deliverance.
Gill: David stood upon and trampled the carcass in just contempt of the blasphemer.
Keil and Delitzsch: he cut off the head with the giant's own sword; Ellicott: Israel felt the Invisible King with them and charged with an irresistible shout.
Wesley: David must have been of considerable strength to wield the sword; 'God is glorified when his enemies are cut off with their own sword'; Clarke: the Philistines were panic-struck.
Henry: cut off with his own sword — God makes the enemy's tongue fall upon him (Ps. 64:8).
Gill: the men of Israel and Judah rose from their trenches to pursue.
Ellicott sorts the topography — 'a valley' without the article, the Septuagint's 'to Gath,' and Shaaraim as a Judahite town or the gateway of Ekron.
Gill: the pursuit ended when the survivors reached their fortified cities.
Keil and Delitzsch: the Israelites plundered the camp, the verb one of hot pursuit (cf. Gen. 31:36).
Henry: they seized the baggage, plundered the tents, and enriched themselves with the spoil.
Gill: the head carried to Jerusalem, likely to the terror of the Jebusites on Zion.
Keil and Delitzsch and Ellicott resolve the apparent anachronism (Jebus/Zion vs the city; cf. Josh. 15:63; Judg. 1:21) and weigh the meaning of 'his tent.'
Clarke notes only that the verse is among the Septuagint's omissions.

debates.question: What is meant by David putting the armour 'in his tent'?

sides.position: his own dwelling at Bethlehem
the tabernacle of Jehovah (Abarbanel), later 'pitched' in David's city (cf. Acts 15:16)

schools.summary: Keil and Delitzsch read the question as pluperfect and about family, not identity; Ellicott resolves the difficulty by two overlapping documents woven together.
Wesley: David had been long absent and Saul's distemper made him forgetful; Abner, in the camp, had never noticed him.
Gill: the inquiry concerns David's family, not his person.

debates.question: How can Saul and Abner seem not to know David after chapter 16?

sides.position: two independent traditions (music and combat) genuinely combined by the compiler
Saul forgot through distemper and David's long absence; Abner never knew him

schools.summary: Gill: the same question, Saul desiring to know of what family the youth was.
Keil and Delitzsch: Abner met the returning hero to congratulate him and brought him before Saul.
Gill: David came carrying the head in triumph, amid the people's acclamations.
Keil and Delitzsch: Abner led the returning victor into the royal presence.
Henry: Saul, melancholy and forgetful, asks whose son he is; David is introduced with far greater advantages than before.
Gill: the question is of David's family, not his person.
Keil and Delitzsch: David plainly said more (cf. 18:1); the rest went unrecorded as bearing no lasting result.
Clarke presses the difficulty as 'exceedingly strange' and, with Kennicott and Pilkington, judges the section a rabbinic interpolation absent from the Septuagint; he closes on the reality of giants.

debates.question: Is the section (vv.12-31, 55-58) an interpolation or genuine text?

sides.position: a later rabbinic interpolation, absent from the Vatican Septuagint
genuine text drawn from contemporary documents
